en.extract:
The blood pheasant (Ithaginis cruentus) or blood partridge is a galliforme bird in the pheasant family Phasianidae and the only species in the genus Ithaginis. It is a relatively small, short-tailed pheasant that is widespread in the lower Himalayas ranging across North and East India, Nepal, Bhutan, South China and northern Myanmar. It has been classified as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List since 2009, and the global blood pheasant population is thought to be stable.
The blood pheasant is the state bird of Sikkim, India.


Description

The blood pheasant has the size of a small fowl, about 17 in (43 cm) in length with a short, convex, very strong black bill, feathered between bill and eye, and a small crest of variously coloured feathers. The colour of the plumage above is dark ash, with white shafts, the coverts of the wings various tinged with green, with broad strokes of white through the length of each feather, the feathers of the chin deep crimson; on the breast, belly, and sides, feathers are lance-shaped, of various length, the tips green with crimson margins, collectively resembling dashes of blood scattered on the breast and belly. The tail consists of 12 subequal feathers, shafts white, rounded, the ends whitish, the coverts a rich crimson red.
Both males and females have red feet and a distinct ring of bare skin around the eye that typically is crimson colored, but is orange in a few subspecies. Females are more uniformly colored, being overall dull brown and often with some gray to the nape. Although some of the subspecies that have been described are highly distinctive, others are not, and some variation appears to be clinal. Consequently, the number of valid subspecies is disputed, with various authorities recognizing between 11 and 15. They mainly vary in the plumage of the males, especially the amount of red or black to the throat, forehead, neck, chest and tail, and the presence or absence of rufous in the wings.


Taxonomy
Twelve  subspecies have been described:

I. c. affinis (CW Beebe, 1912) – Sikkim region in India
I. c. beicki (Mayr and Birckhead, 1937) – Beick's blood pheasant – north-central China
I. c. berezowskii (Bianchi, 1903) – Berezovski's blood pheasant – mountains of central China
I. c. clarkei (Rothschild, 1920) – Clarke's blood pheasant – southwest China
I. c. cruentus (Hardwicke, 1821) – Himalayan blood pheasant – northern Nepal to northwestern Bhutan
I. c. geoffroyi (Verreaux, 1867) – Geoffroy's blood pheasant – western China and southeast Tibet
I. c. kuseri (Beebe, 1912) – Kuser's blood pheasant – upper Assam in India and southeast Tibet
I. c. marionae (Mayr, 1941) – Mrs. Vernay's blood pheasant – mountains of southwest China and northeast Myanmar
I. c. michaelis (Bianchi, 1903) – Bianchi's blood pheasant – north-central China
I. c. rocki (Riley, 1925) – Rock's blood pheasant – southwestern China
I. c. sinensis (David, 1873) – David's blood pheasant – central China
I. c. tibetanus (Baker, 1914) – Tibetan blood pheasant – eastern Bhutan and southern Tibet


Distribution and habitat
Blood pheasants live in the mountains of Nepal, Sikkim, northern Myanmar, Tibet, and central and south-central China, where they prefer coniferous or mixed forests and scrub areas near the snowline. They move their range depending on the seasons, and are found at higher elevations during the summer. With snow increasing in fall and winter, they move to lower elevations.


Ecology
Unlike the common pheasant, the blood pheasant is monogamous. Breeding season begins at the end of April when males begin their courtship by displaying in front of females by flying at each other breast-to-breast, biting wattles, or performing high leaps with kicks toward the other's bill. Mate choice by females depends on factors like tail length, length of the ear tufts, and the presence of black points in the wattle, but neither wattle size or color nor the brightness of the plumage affect the females' choice. 
The blood pheasant reaches sexual maturity in one year. In early May, mature females begin nesting under paddy straw heaps surrounded by trees, in brush, small caves, or in tree holes near the ground. They excavate shallow pot-like nests and line them with moss, pine needles, and feathers. The female lays 4-14 yellowish-white eggs with brown speckles, at intervals of two or three days. During the 27-33 days of egg incubation, the male is responsible for defending the territory against other male pheasants.
The blood pheasant's distribution in inhospitable high-elevation environments has required adaptation to hypoxia and high levels of ultraviolet radiation.
Blood pheasants move with the snow line when foraging, feeding on moss, ferns, pine shoots, and lichens.


Conservation
The species is currently classified as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List. However, more than half the population is considered to be threatened by habitat loss and fragmentation, illegal hunting and human disturbance. Much of the distribution range of pheasants in Southwest China overlaps with the habitat of the giant panda, and livestock grazing has become the most prevalent human disturbance across associated protection areas. The blood pheasant's ground-nesting habits and relatively long incubation period make it especially vulnerable to trampling and habitat degradation by grazers.


References


External links

The '''blood pheasant''' ('''''Ithaginis cruentus''''') or '''blood partridge''' is a [[Galliformes|galliforme]] bird in the [[pheasant]] family Phasianidae and the [[Monotypic taxon|only species]] in the [[genus]] '''''Ithaginis'''''. It is a relatively small, short-tailed pheasant that is widespread in the lower [[Himalayas]] ranging across [[North India|North]] and [[East India]], [[Nepal]], [[Bhutan]], [[South China]] and northern [[Myanmar]]. It has been classified as [[Least-concern species|Least Concern]] on the [[IUCN Red List]] since 2009, and the global blood pheasant population is thought to be stable.<ref name=iucn/>

The blood pheasant is the [[List of Indian state birds|state bird]] of [[Sikkim]], India.<ref name=Kazmierczak>{{cite book |last1=Kazmierczak |first1=K. |last2=Singh |first2=R. |title=A Birdwatchers' Guide to India |year=1998 |location=London, UK |publisher=Prion |page=137 |isbn=978-1-871104-08-0}}</ref>

== Description ==
[[File:Blood Pheasant female from Lachung North Sikkim India 14032019.jpg|thumb|Female in Sikkim, [[India]].]]
The blood pheasant has the size of a small [[fowl]], about {{convert|17|in|cm|abbr=on}} in length with a short, convex, very strong black bill, feathered between bill and eye, and a small crest of variously coloured [[feather]]s. The colour of the plumage above is dark ash, with white shafts, the [[Covert (feather)|coverts]] of the wings various tinged with green, with broad strokes of white through the length of each feather, the feathers of the chin deep crimson; on the breast, belly, and sides, feathers are lance-shaped, of various length, the tips green with crimson margins, collectively resembling dashes of [[blood]] scattered on the breast and belly. The tail consists of 12 subequal feathers, shafts white, rounded, the ends whitish, the coverts a rich crimson red.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Hardwicke, T. |year=1821 |title=Descriptions of a Wild Dog of Sumatra, a new Species of Viverra, and a new Species of Pheasant. |journal=The Transactions of the Linnean Society of London |volume= XIII |pages=235–238 |publisher=Linnean Society of London |url=https://archive.org/stream/transactionsofli13lond#page/n301/mode/2up}}</ref>

Both males and females have red feet and a distinct ring of bare skin around the eye that typically is crimson colored, but is orange in a few subspecies. Females are more uniformly colored, being overall dull brown and often with some gray to the nape. Although some of the [[subspecies]] that have been described are highly distinctive, others are not, and some variation appears to be [[Cline (biology)|clinal]]. Consequently, the number of valid subspecies is disputed, with various authorities recognizing between 11 and 15. They mainly vary in the [[plumage]] of the males, especially the amount of red or black to the throat, forehead, neck, chest and tail, and the presence or absence of [[rufous]] in the wings.<ref name="johnsgard99">Johnsgard, P. A. (1999) The Pheasants of the World: Biology and Natural History. Washington, D.C: Smithsonian Institution Press</ref>

==Taxonomy==
Twelve  [[subspecies]] have been described:<ref>del Hoyo, J., Collar, N.J., Christie, D.A., Elliott, A. and Fishpool, L.D.C. 2014. HBW and BirdLife International Illustrated Checklist of the Birds of the World. Volume 1: Non-passerines. Lynx Edicions BirdLife International, Barcelona, Spain and Cambridge, UK.</ref>
* ''I. c. affinis'' <small>([[William Beebe|CW Beebe]], 1912)</small> – Sikkim region in India
* ''I. c. beicki'' <small>([[Ernst Mayr|Mayr]] and Birckhead, 1937)</small> – Beick's blood pheasant – north-central China
* ''I. c. berezowskii'' <small>([[Valentin Lvovich Bianchi|Bianchi]], 1903)</small> – Berezovski's blood pheasant – mountains of central China
* ''I. c. clarkei'' <small>([[Walter Rothschild, 2nd Baron Roth-schild|Rothschild]], 1920)</small> – Clarke's blood pheasant – southwest China
* ''I. c. cruentus'' <small>(Hardwicke, 1821)</small> – Himalayan blood pheasant – northern Nepal to northwestern Bhutan
* ''I. c. geoffroyi'' <small>([[Jules Verreaux|Verreaux]], 1867)</small> – Geoffroy's blood pheasant – western China and southeast Tibet
* ''I. c. kuseri'' <small>(Beebe, 1912)</small> – Kuser's blood pheasant – upper [[Assam]] in India and southeast Tibet
* ''I. c. marionae'' <small>(Mayr, 1941)</small> – Mrs. Vernay's blood pheasant – mountains of southwest China and northeast [[Myanmar]]
* ''I. c. michaelis'' <small>(Bianchi, 1903)</small> – Bianchi's blood pheasant – north-central China
* ''I. c. rocki'' <small>([[Joseph Harvey Riley|Riley]], 1925)</small> – Rock's blood pheasant – southwestern China
* ''I. c. sinensis'' <small>([[Armand David|David]], 1873)</small> – David's blood pheasant – central China
* ''I. c. tibetanus'' <small>([[E. C. Stuart Baker|Baker]], 1914)</small> – Tibetan blood pheasant – eastern Bhutan and southern Tibet

== Distribution and habitat ==
Blood pheasants live in the mountains of [[Nepal]], Sikkim, northern Myanmar, [[Tibet]], and central and south-central China, where they prefer [[conifer]]ous or mixed [[forest]]s and [[scrubland|scrub]] areas near the [[snowline]]. They move their range depending on the seasons, and are found at higher elevations during the summer. With snow increasing in fall and winter, they move to lower elevations.<ref name="johnsgard99"/>

==Ecology==
Unlike the [[common pheasant]], the blood pheasant is [[Monogamy in animals|monogamous]].<ref name="auto">{{cite journal |last1=Zhou |first1=C. |last2=Liu |first2=Y. |last3=Qiao |first3=L. |last4=Liu |first4=Y. |last5=Yang |first5=N. |last6=Meng |first6=Y. |last7=Yue |first7=B. |name-list-style=amp |title=The draft genome of the blood pheasant (''Ithaginis cruentus''): Phylogeny and high-altitude adaptation |journal=Ecology and Evolution |date=2020 |volume=10 |issue=20 |pages=11440–11452 |doi=10.1002/ece3.6782 |pmid=33144976 |pmc=7593199 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Breeding season begins at the end of April when males begin their courtship by displaying in front of females by flying at each other breast-to-breast, biting wattles, or performing high leaps with kicks toward the other's bill.<ref name="auto1">{{cite web |last1=Switzer |first1=C. |title=''Phasianus colchicus'' (common pheasant) |url=https://animaldiversity.org/accounts/Phasianus_colchicus/ |website=Animal Diversity Web |language=en}}</ref> [[Mate choice]] by females depends on factors like tail length, length of the [[ear tuft]]s, and the presence of black points in the [[Wattle (anatomy)|wattle]], but neither wattle size or color nor the brightness of the plumage affect the females' choice.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Mateos |first1=C. |last2=Carranza |first2=J. |name-list-style=amp |title=Female choice for morphological features of male ring-necked pheasants |journal=Animal Behaviour |date=1995 |volume=49 |issue=3 |pages=737–748 |doi=10.1016/0003-3472(95)80206-1 |s2cid=53159955 }}</ref> 

The blood pheasant reaches [[sexual maturity]] in one year.<ref name="auto1"/> In early May, mature females begin nesting under paddy straw heaps surrounded by trees, in brush, small caves, or in tree holes near the ground.<ref name="auto2">{{cite web |title=Blood Pheasants {{!}} Beauty of Birds |url=https://www.beautyofbirds.com/bloodpheasants.html |website=www.beautyofbirds.com|date=16 September 2021 }}</ref> They excavate shallow pot-like nests and line them with moss, pine needles, and feathers. The female lays 4-14 yellowish-white eggs with brown speckles, at intervals of two or three days. During the 27-33 days of [[egg incubation]], the male is responsible for defending the territory against other male pheasants.<ref name="auto2"/>

The blood pheasant's distribution in inhospitable high-elevation environments has required adaptation to [[hypoxia (environmental)|hypoxia]] and high levels of [[ultraviolet]] radiation.<ref name="auto"/>

Blood pheasants move with the [[snow line]] when foraging, feeding on moss, ferns, pine shoots, and lichens.{{cn|date=March 2021}}

==Conservation==
The species is currently classified as [[Least Concern]] on the [[IUCN Red List]].<ref name=iucn/> However, more than half the population is considered to be threatened by [[habitat loss]] and fragmentation, illegal hunting and human disturbance.<ref name=Fan_al2020>{{cite journal |author1=Fan, F. |name-list-style=amp |author2=Bu, H. |author3=McShea, W.J. |author4=Shen, X. |author5=Li, B.V. |author6=Li, S. |title=Seasonal habitat use and activity patterns of blood pheasant ''Ithaginis cruentusbe'' in the presence of free-ranging livestock |journal=Global Ecology and Conservation |date=2020 |volume=23 |article-number=e01155 |doi=10.1016/j.gecco.2020.e01155 |doi-access=free}}</ref> Much of the distribution range of pheasants in Southwest China overlaps with the habitat of the [[giant panda]], and livestock grazing has become the most prevalent human disturbance across associated protection areas.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Wei |first1=W. |last2=Swaisgood |first2=R. R. |last3=Dai |first3=Q. |last4=Yang |first4=Z. |last5=Yuan |first5=S. |last6=Owen |first6=M. A. |last7=Pilfold |first7=N. W. |last8=Yang |first8=X. |last9=Gu |first9=X. |last10=Zhou |first10=H. |last11=Han |first11=H. |last12=Zhang |first12=J. |last13=Hong |first13=M. |last14=Zhang |first14=Z. |title=Giant panda distributional and habitat-use shifts in a changing landscape |journal=Conservation Letters |date=2018 |volume=11 |issue=6 |article-number=e12575 |doi=10.1111/conl.12575 |doi-access=free }}</ref> The blood pheasant's ground-nesting habits and relatively long incubation period make it especially vulnerable to trampling and habitat degradation by grazers.<ref name=Fan_al2020/><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Jia |first1=C.-X. |last2=Sun |first2=Y.-H. |last3=Swenson |first3=J. E. |title=Unusual Incubation Behavior and Embryonic Tolerance of Hypothermia by the Blood Pheasant (''Ithaginis cruentus'') |journal=The Auk |date=2010 |volume=127 |issue=4 |pages=926–931 |doi=10.1525/auk.2010.09254 |s2cid=85721087 |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/276946460|doi-access=free }}</ref>

zh.extract:
血雉（学名：Ithaginis cruentus），又名血雞，是雉科血雉属中的唯一一種，它是锡金的邦鸟，中國國家二級保護動物。它有12个亚种。
血雉的名称来自于雄鸟胸部、颈部和面部的鲜红色羽毛。雌鸟的羽毛色为统一的昏暗的红棕色。雄鸟与雌鸟的眼周围都有一圈没有羽毛的绯红色的皮肤。血雉的足是红色的。不同亚种的区分在于它们身上红色羽毛与黑色羽毛的数量的不同。
血雉生活在尼泊尔、印度锡金邦、缅甸北部、中国西藏及西北部的山区。一般它们喜爱雪线附近的针叶林、混合林和灌木林。夏天它
们比较喜欢高处，秋天和冬天它们随雪线的降低而移向低处。


亚种
现时血雉有12种亚种:

I. c. affinis (CW Beebe, 1912)
I. c. beicki (Mayr and Birckhead, 1937)
I. c. berezowskii (Bianchi, 1903)
I. c. clarkei (Rothschild, 1920)
I. c. cruentus (Hardwicke, 1821)
I. c. geoffroyi (Verreaux, 1867)
I. c. kuseri (Beebe, 1912)
I. c. marionae (Mayr, 1941)
I. c. michaelis (Bianchi, 1903)
I. c. rocki (Riley, 1925)
I. c. sinensis (David, 1873)
I. c. tibetanus (ECS Baker, 1914)


保育狀況
其被列為《瀕危野生動植物種國際貿易公約》附錄二的物種，被限制出口及貿易。
